comments: No Friday Night Connection due to the live set replays.

track_listing.track: Boom Boom Out Go The Lights

transcript.text: Well, hello there. This is TV on the radio here, Thomas the Vance, and welcome once again to the Friday Rock Show.
Right, tonight we revisit the scene of the crime with the first of two programs that features the groups that we recorded and
broadcast last summer at the Reading Festival. Now the consequence of that is that the Friday night connection is shelved.
But don't be disappointed about that because this week you can relive the ace activities of the Pat Travis band, Magnum, Samson and Gillen.
But we start our reading revisited doubleheader with five numbers from Iron Maiden and here they are
Mr. Stevie Harris.
The lead guitar, the glom bomber, Mr. Dave Murray.
This is a song we'd like to dedicate from us to you.
This is a song called Iron.
Iron Maiden, those bunch of loony West Ham supporters
as they were at the Reading Festival last year.
At the Traxy Herb were Iron Maiden, Transylvania, Running Free,
Remember Tomorrow and Prowler.
I think it's time to mention a few people who are into Iron Maiden.
I've got a nice letter here from Ian Ireland at No. 7 Ashdown Court,
north over close Westbury, down there in Bristol,
demands that I mention Dave Guest and Dave Potter,
John Gratlin, Tony Fisk, Rob White, Steve Trevis,
and all the rest of the people that he knows at Bristol Grammar School.
A little letter that's from a gentleman who signs himself A2
of No. 26 Arthur O. Cliftonville in Margate,
definitely into Iron Maiden.
...who just two days ago emigrated to South Africa,
wanted me to be very kind and mention some friends of hers
that she's left behind in Glasgow.
That's Alison and Chris and Len and Susan and Jackie and Gus and Diane and Brian in Glasgow.
And also Bob in Birmingham.
I'm sure that Louise Rain is going to have a great time when she gets over there to South Africa.
Probably there now.
But one thing she isn't going to hear is our second band on the program tonight.
And that particular band is the amazing Driving Gillen.
In one of rock's greatest all-time classics, I'm Barry Manilow.
That, of course, was Gillen, as recorded at Reading last year.
Smoke on the Water, Vengeance, Mr. Universe, and Unchain Your Brain.
And just a few of the people who wanted to hear that particular set by Gillan,
let me just mention them.
Jean, who lives at Shortlands in Bromley, wants me to say hello to Sarah,
who actually happens to be a Barry Manilow fan.
Helen Blair wants me to wish a very happy 13th birthday to Matthew Blair.
The birthday's coming up on the 26th of February.
Ian Bishop, who lives in Oakdale, a block wood in Gwent,
demands that I say hello to Martin and Deb and Phil and Fat Bartlett.
and Ted and Wendy and Catherine and Andrew and Dottie
and Horace and Haddon and Cheery and Danish.
Got a little letter here from a gentleman
who normally is serving upon the British Respect,
which is a BP tanker.
He wanted me to say hello to lots of his mates over there in Belfast,
all the people that go to the Pound Club,
most especially Steve Foreman and his bird, Pat,
and Ricky and George, and David and Billy and Bert,
not forgetting Shubbert and Betty.
Finally, a letter here signed Richie Blackmore,
balamina northern ireland don't believe a word of it wanted me to say hello to glenn and ethnic
vortex roderick and also his dog you're listening to the friday rock show reading repeats part one
and now here's four good numbers by the band called samson thunderstake chris aylmer bruce
bruce and paul samson the band called samson four tracks there as recorded at reading last
year hammerhead walking out on you vice versa and hard times talking about hard times just
time for one letter here. This comes from a lady by the name of Maureen Lyons, who lives
in Thornhill in Southampton. She wants me to say hello to a gentleman who is lost to
her every Friday night, and that's a gentleman by the name of Steve Graham, who lives in
Grendon Underwood in Buckinghamshire. He had a birthday on the 17th. Today is Maureen's
birthday, and he's not with her because he's listening to the Friday Rock Show, and I hope
thoroughly enjoying it. I'll tell you something, Maureen, you can have him back actually after
midnight but now here's magnum the album is called chase the dragon this song is called sacred hour
my mind for this black sacred hour is still i've been moved for so long by this strange
fascination here as i stand all alone in complete concentration
Nice to see you.
We're going to play one of our oldest songs.
This is a song on a live EP we had out a few months back.
This is called All of My Life.
Kingdom of Madness, Invasion, All of My Life and Sacred Hour.
Plus, of course, the first time that they played Changes, Magnum from last year's Reading Festival.
And we finished tonight's programme with one of the highlights of last year's festival.
It was announced like this.
top that if you possibly can there you have the pat travis band boom boom out go the lights born
under a bad sign hooked on music and rock and roll suzy and that was our friday rock show reading
repeats part one i hope you enjoyed our bands tonight the pat travis band magnum samson gillan
and iron maiden now following the time tone for midnight the 275 285 transmitters will be closing
down till tomorrow morning a quick word here about our engineers who work hard on these sessions
when we record them,
Ted DiBono, Mike Robinson and Dave Day.
This programme is produced by Tony Wilson.
He refuses to talk tonight.
He's getting stroppy.
Next week, we'll have Slay, Def Leppard, Budgie, Crocus and Whitesnake
in our Friday Rock Show Reading Repeats Part 2.
This is TV on the Radio, Thomas the Vance,
getting off it till next week.
God bless, take care and good night.
It's midnight and we welcome VHF listeners
who've now joined us on Radio 2.
This is Vivienne Stewart in the Midnight Newsroom.
The headlines, three Labour MPs who support the Council for Social Democracy
say they're resigning from the party.
Miners' leaders in four key areas have decided to end industrial action over pit closures
and how Lady Diana Spencer slipped through Heathrow Airport
and into a controversy over security.
Three Labour MPs who support the Council for Social Democracy
have announced they're resigning from the party.
They're Tom Ellis, Ian Rigglesworth and Richard Crawshaw,
who's also resigning as Deputy Speaker of the Commons.
They're the first MPs to make a complete break from Labour over the issue.
Another MP, Tom Bradley, has told his constituency party
he won't be standing as a Labour candidate in the next election.
From Westminster, Brian Curtois.
Mr Bradley said nothing tonight about his future
as a member of Labour's National Executive Committee,
but he looks set to resign.
He said that the NEC and the party's annual conference
had knocked the living daylights out of decent, well-established party practices.
He would be morally compelled to join a new party if the council is formed into one,
and that's expected to happen towards the end of next month.
Peter William Sutcliffe has been sent for trial by magistrates at Dewsbury in West Yorkshire.
He's accused of murdering 13 women and attempting to murder seven others.
Sutcliffe, a 35-year-old lorry driver from Bradford,
was committed in custody to Leeds Crown Court.
Kent miners are now standing alone in defiance of their National Union executives' instruction to return to work.
They're concerned about the future of Snowdown Colliery near Dover.
But miners' leaders in four key areas, Scotland, South Wales, Yorkshire and Derbyshire,
have decided to call off their campaign of industrial action.
Here's Nicholas Jones.
The Yorkshire leader, Mr Arthur Scargill, was in a difficult position.
As the left's candidate for the presidency to succeed Mr Joe Gormley, who's retiring,
Mr Scargill knew his position could be damaged
if he continued to campaign for unofficial strike action
in defiance of the majority decision at yesterday's executive meeting in London
which called for an immediate return to work.
In Scotland, where nearly half the men were already back at work,
the President, Mr Mick McGarvey, regretted the decision to end the strike.
And in South Wales, where the unofficial strike began on Tuesday,
the local President, Mr Emlyn Williams,
warned that his members still had reservations over the government's assurances.
The British Steel Corporation and GKN, Britain's biggest engineering firm,
are to form a new company employing 5,000 people.
It's hoped the combination of public and private enterprise
will give a boost to the flagging steel industry.
Basque separatist guerrillas who kidnapped three foreign consuls
from their homes in Spain have now issued a series of demands.
They want full home rule, the release of Basque political prisoners
and the closing of a nuclear power plant near Bilbao.
Gunmen in Northern Ireland have attacked the driver of a school bus
in County Fermanagh near the border with the Irish Republic
The driver, a former member of the Ulster Defence Regiment
was only slightly hurt
The attack happened just after he dropped off the last children
from the bus on a route which serves both sides of the border
Prince Charles' friend Lady Diana Spencer
stepped off a plane at Heathrow Airport tonight
and into the centre of controversy over airport security
She apparently managed to escape the usual customs checks
after flying back from a holiday in Australia,
and the British Airports Authority is investigating how she did it.
Here's Chris West.
Lady Diana arrived back unexpectedly from a two-week holiday in Australia
where her stepfather has a sheep farm.
It appears that while her fellow passengers
queued to go through the usual formalities at Heathrow Immigration Control,
Lady Diana was whisked off the tarmac
and into a car by a high-ranking airline official,
who then escorted her out of the airport.
Now the British Airports Authority is asking
how she managed to bypass the normal channels
and whether, in fact, she went through customs clearance at all.
The Home Office said tonight
that everyone coming into Britain was subject to checks.
It was possible to arrange for these to be made on the aircraft itself
before landing, but they couldn't confirm
whether this had been the case with Lady Diana.
From France come reports of a gang of thieves
who really put one over on their victims.
The gang struck on a night train near Strasbourg
and using sleeping gas to put passengers in some compartments
well and truly out for the count,
the passengers got a very rude awakening.
They discovered that their wallets, jewellery, cameras
and other valuables had vanished, as had the thieves.
Soccer.
The European champions Nottingham Forest
have been fined nearly £7,000
for displaying advertisements on players' shirts
and during a match in Spain.
The fine was imposed by the European Football Union, UEFA,
and related to a game against Valencia in the European Super Cup competition in December.
And tonight's results here at home.
Division 1, Birmingham 4, Norwich City, nil.
Division 2, Cardiff City, nil, Notts County, one.
And Division 4, Doncaster Rovers, three, York City, two.
Now the weather forecast.
It'll be dry and bright in most places, though there may be some snow showers in the south-east.
More general snow will reach western parts during the day.
Well, that's all for now. We'll have our next news at 1am.
